sections.heading: Jarvis AI
sections.text: A personal voice + text AI assistant for macOS. Jarvis combines local-first inference, cloud escalation when needed, persistent memory, self-learning, live browser and system control, and a PyQt6 desktop UI.

sections.heading: Features
sections.text: - **Voice + text interface** — speech-to-text input, ElevenLabs TTS output, and a desktop chat UI - **Local-first model routing** — Ollama handles private everyday requests first, with GPT-mini, Haiku, Sonnet, or Opus used only when the task warrants the extra cost - **Persistent memory** — remembers facts, preferences, projects, and recent context from local JSON stores - **Local skills** — lightweight skill metadata stays cheap to load, while full SKILL.md instructions and references load only for the active request - **Task-scoped conversation context** — Jarvis keeps only the active task in prompt history, rotates between unrelated requests, and compacts older turns into a short carry-over summary - **Local markdown vault** — indexed markdown files in vault/ can be searched and selectively injected into the current request before Jarvis grows prompt context or escalates outward - **Wiki compiler** — raw markdown in vault/raw/ can be compiled into cleaned topic pages and cross-topic indexes for cheaper local retrieval - **Self-learning** — background knowledge feed, fact extraction, and daily reflection - **Live browser control** — open sites, search, summarize the current page, navigate back and forward, reload, and click visible links or buttons - **System control** — volume, brightness, screenshots, app launch, lock screen, clipboard readout, and shell commands - **Admin command path** — can run a terminal command through the native macOS administrator prompt when explicitly asked - **Google integration** — Calendar and Gmail read/create/send via OAuth2 - **Meeting overlay** — floating HUD during calls with live transcript, real-time AI suggestions, and screen scan; invisible to screen share - **Webcam + screen vision** — image and screen analysis from the camera and desktop - **Self-improvement** — Jarvis can inspect and rewrite parts of its own source, validate generated Python, back up originals, and apply changes atomically - **Stealth mode** — windows hidden from screen share using macOS APIs

sections.heading: Requirements
sections.text: - macOS - Python 3.12+ - for local model support - (optional) for capturing call audio in meeting mode

sections.heading: Setup
sections.text: 1. Clone the repo: 2. Create and activate a virtual environment: 3. Install dependencies: 4. Create a .env file in the project root: 5. Optional: add credentials.json from Google Cloud Console for Calendar/Gmail OAuth. 6. Install and pull the recommended local models: 7. Grant macOS permissions when prompted: - Accessibility, so global hotkeys and input automation can work - Contacts, so first-run iMessage contact lookup can succeed - Microphone, camera, and screen recording as needed for voice, webcam, and screen analysis - Automation permissions for controlling apps like Safari, Messages, and Terminal

sections.heading: Running
sections.text: ```bash

sections.heading: GUI mode (default)
sections.text: ./run.sh

sections.heading: Headless / terminal-only
sections.text: ./run.sh --no-ui `` Jarvis exposes a local API while running: - GET /status — current mode and local-model availability - POST /chat — chat with Jarvis - GET /context — inspect current prompt/session footprint and recent request context stats - GET /vault — inspect the current local vault index status - POST /vault/build — compile raw markdown into wiki pages and rebuild the vault indexes - GET /memory — inspect saved memory - POST /mode — switch local, cloud, or auto`

sections.heading: Skills
sections.text: Jarvis now supports a local skill layer under skills/: - skills/index.json is the L1 metadata index used for cheap relevance checks - skills/<skill_id>/SKILL.md holds the full L2 skill instructions - skills/<skill_id>/references/ holds L3 reference files that load only when that skill is active The first built-in skills are: - browser_execution for live browser navigation and page-action recovery - personal_context for Aman-specific answers grounded in memory and eval signals - self_improvement for evidence-gated self-editing behavior This keeps the baseline prompt smaller while still letting Jarvis load deeper guidance when a request actually needs it.

sections.heading: Hotkeys
sections.text: All hotkeys use Cmd + Option and work system-wide, even during screen share. | Hotkey | Action | |---|---| | ⌘⌥J | Capture screen → Jarvis analyzes it | | ⌘⌥K | Capture webcam frame → Jarvis analyzes it | | ⌘⌥L | Read clipboard → Jarvis responds | | ⌘⌥M | Toggle Smart Listen (meeting audio) | | ⌘⌥O | Toggle Meeting Overlay HUD | | ⌘⌥; | Toggle Jarvis window visibility |

sections.heading: Configuration
sections.text: All model identifiers and the system prompt live in config.py. Change models there, not inline. Model routing mode can be switched at runtime via natural language: - *"switch to cloud mode"* - *"switch to local mode"* - *"switch to auto mode"* Current recommended local defaults: - llama3.1:8b for general local conversation - qwen2.5-coder:7b for coding tasks - mistral for stronger local reasoning auto mode is the default and is intended to keep API usage low without forcing everything through weaker local models. Jarvis now also tracks prompt footprint per request. /chat responses include a context object with the active session id, carried summary size, prompt-size estimate, and rotation count so you can see when context is growing.

sections.heading: Vault
sections.text: Jarvis now includes a local markdown vault: - vault/raw/ for raw source material - vault/wiki/ for cleaned topic pages - vault/indexes/ for generated indexes - vault/outputs/ for generated reports and artifacts Use phrases like refresh the vault index, search the vault for X, or what's in your local knowledge base. Jarvis also searches the vault automatically for knowledge-seeking requests and injects only the most relevant snippets into the active request. You can also say build the vault wiki or compile the wiki. That runs the deterministic wiki_builder.py pipeline, which turns files in vault/raw/ into compiled pages under vault/wiki/compiled/ and refreshes the topic and keyword indexes in vault/indexes/.

sections.heading: Brain Relevance
sections.text: - the product surface described here should stay aligned with [[80 Jarvis Roadmap]] - runtime and routing claims here should be checked against [[78 AI Runtime Agent Engineering Principles]] - major shifts in these promises should be captured in [[70 Jarvis Decision Log]] and [[91 Vault Changelog]] - vault-specific changes should follow [[04 Capture Workflow]] instead of drifting into ad hoc structure

sections.heading: Self-Improve Safety
sections.text: Jarvis can modify parts of its own source, but the apply path is guarded: - generated Python is syntax-validated before any file is changed - the original file is backed up to .jarvis_backups/ - writes go through a temporary file and atomic replace - Jarvis tells you to restart after a successful self-improve run so modules reload cleanly

sections.heading: Security
sections.text: - API keys are loaded from .env and never hardcoded - credentials.json and token.json are gitignored - memory, knowledge, backup, and session files stay local - meeting overlay is invisible to screen share via NSWindowSharingNone

sections.heading: Jarvis Vault Strategy
sections.text: Related brain notes: [[04 Capture Workflow]], [[70 Jarvis Decision Log]], [[78 AI Runtime Agent Engineering Principles]], [[80 Jarvis Roadmap]], [[91 Vault Changelog]], [[93 Vault Maintenance]] Jarvis should use a local markdown vault before it grows prompt context or relies on long conversational carry-over. The vault should stay inspectable and cheap. Raw source material belongs in one place, compiled topic pages belong in another, and cross-topic indexes should be regenerated from local files instead of being hidden in model context. When a request can be answered from local markdown, Jarvis should load only the smallest relevant snippets for that request. The vault is not a giant prompt dump. It is a searchable local knowledge layer.

sections.heading: Brain Schema
sections.text: Purpose: keep the Jarvis brain queryable, linkable, and durable without depending on any Obsidian plugin runtime. Linked notes: [[00 Home]], [[02 Brain Dashboard]], [[04 Capture Workflow]], [[06 Vault Support Hub]], [[70 Jarvis Decision Log]], [[90 Task Hub]], [[93 Vault Maintenance]]

sections.heading: Principle
sections.text: Borrow the discipline from Dataview-style vaults, but keep the core brain usable as plain markdown. That means: - metadata should be predictable - notes should still read well without any plugin - tasks should still work as plain markdown checkboxes - canvases should use the open .canvas format - Jarvis should own the capture contract itself instead of relying on plugin scripting

sections.heading: Recommended Frontmatter
sections.text: Use concise YAML frontmatter on new operational notes, new templates, and any high-value curated notes we touch going forward. Preferred fields: - id: optional stable identifier for notes Jarvis may patch surgically later - type: what kind of note this is - area: product, career, engineering, security, ops, vault - owner: user, jarvis, or generated - write_policy: curated, append_only, generated, or propose_only - review_required: true when Jarvis should stage or propose instead of silently updating canonical content - scope: optional memory scope such as personal, project, or system - status: active, draft, evergreen, archived - source: repo, resume, claude_export, chatgpt_export, manual - confidence: high, medium, low - created: YYYY-MM-DD - updated: YYYY-MM-DD - version: integer revision marker when a note is actively maintained - tags: short stable labels - related: wikilinks to nearby notes - role_targets: optional list for interview and career notes

sections.heading: Type Suggestions
sections.text: - brain_meta - identity - project - decision - story - playbook - roadmap - task_hub - capture_template - investigation

sections.heading: Ownership Contract
sections.text: - owner: user Canonical human-owned notes. Jarvis may read them freely, but write access should be narrow and intentional. - owner: jarvis Operational notes Jarvis is allowed to maintain directly, usually append-first surfaces like inboxes or queues. - owner: generated Deterministic build outputs, compiled notes, or indexes. Do not patch these manually through curator actions.

sections.heading: Write Policy Contract
sections.text: - write_policy: curated High-value canonical notes. Prefer explicit user-directed edits and bounded heading-level updates only when the target is clear. When review_required: true, native curator updates should stage into candidates instead of writing directly into canon. - write_policy: append_only Safe operational notes. Jarvis may append tasks, updates, or queue items without rewriting the whole note. - write_policy: generated Generated artifacts. Update the source or generator, not the output file. - write_policy: propose_only Jarvis should route changes into [[92 Agent Inbox]] or vault/wiki/candidates/ first, then promote them deliberately.

sections.heading: Candidate Layer Contract
sections.text: - vault/wiki/candidates/ is the staging lane between inbox work and canon. - Candidate notes are safe for Jarvis to create and append when a canonical note is propose_only. - Candidate notes should point back to the canonical target clearly and keep proposed updates under a bounded heading. - Promotion from candidate to canon should stay explicit and reviewable. - Promotion should merge only the selected proposed update back into the canonical heading, then leave a durable promotion log in the candidate note.

sections.heading: Task Contract
sections.text: Use plain markdown tasks so the note still works without plugins: - - [ ] Ship mic fix 📅 2026-04-16 #jarvis #voice - - [ ] Verify packaged app 📅 2026-04-16 #packaging - - [x] Distill Anthropic variant #brain #career Rules: - start with a verb - add a due date only when real - keep tags short and stable - link target notes when the task changes a specific artifact

sections.heading: Linking Contract
sections.text: - use direct wikilinks for durable relationships - prefer explicit links over hoping graph proximity will emerge - link a note to the hub note that would logically surface it - when a note changes product direction or memory policy, link it into [[70 Jarvis Decision Log]]

sections.heading: Context-Bundle Contract
sections.text: When Jarvis later builds note bundles, prefer a thin local bridge: - read the note directly - patch the smallest relevant heading or field - pass explicit note refs instead of dumping giant context blobs - prefer related-note bundles over raw top-k transcript fragments

sections.heading: Change-Provenance Contract
sections.text: Major brain changes should leave a durable trace in [[91 Vault Changelog]]. Update the changelog when: - a new durable note family is added - retrieval rules or memory hierarchy change - a major import or distillation pass lands - a new canvas or workflow meaningfully changes how the brain is navigated

sections.heading: Plugin Boundary
sections.text: Good optional accelerators: - Dataview for querying metadata - Tasks for vault-wide task views - QuickAdd or Templater for human capture convenience Do not make Jarvis depend on those plugins to read or write the brain correctly.

sections.heading: Supporting Surfaces
sections.text: - [[06 Vault Support Hub]] is the grouped support-layer anchor for these references. - explains where schema applies across raw, compiled, and curated layers. - is the provenance layer that should stay compatible with this schema. - and are generated read surfaces, not schema owners. - is the human-readable vault contract that should stay aligned with this note.

sections.heading: Promotion Rules
sections.text: Promote material into the curated brain only when it improves one of these: - identity grounding - project continuity - reusable stories - technical playbooks - product decisions - roadmap clarity If it does not improve one of those, keep it in raw sources or a temporary working note.

sections.heading: Mutation Rules
sections.text: When Jarvis gains more direct vault tools later, prefer: - heading-level edits over whole-file rewrites - frontmatter patching over freeform metadata drift - explicit note references over hidden context - template-backed note creation over ad hoc structures - [[92 Agent Inbox]] for open-ended background curation that should stay reviewable before it becomes canonical memory - append_only notes for continuous agent maintenance work - propose_only notes when Jarvis should gather candidate changes under vault/wiki/candidates/ without directly rewriting the canon - explicit promotion when a candidate update is accepted into the canonical note

sections.heading: Major-Change Rule
sections.text: If a capture changes the way Jarvis should reason, route, remember, or present itself, add an entry to [[91 Vault Changelog]] and link the affected note from [[70 Jarvis Decision Log]] or [[80 Jarvis Roadmap]].

sections.text: Purpose: capture durable facts about the user that should shape Jarvis's tone, priorities, and responses. Linked notes: [[20 Projects]], [[30 Preferences]], [[40 Timeline]], [[50 Synthesis]], [[60 Interview Story Bank]]

sections.heading: Core Identity
sections.text: - Name: Aman Imran. - Base: San Francisco Bay Area. - Professional identity: Trust and Safety, AI safety, and security operations leader with 7+ years of experience across abuse prevention, fraud detection, incident response, and global GSOC operations. - Platform background includes YouTube, Meta, Google Play, TikTok, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and an Anthropic-aligned AI safety operations role through Allied Universal. - Strongest domain areas: child safety enforcement, content integrity QA, adversarial AI misuse detection, jailbreak and prompt-injection investigation, classifier calibration, fraud and abuse analysis, and high-severity incident response.

sections.heading: Working Style
sections.text: - Prefers direct, iterative collaboration over vague high-level advice. - Wants defensible, interview-ready claims tied to real evidence and real metrics. - Pushes back on fabricated numbers and inflated positioning. - Likes surgical edits that preserve working structure, especially in resumes, LaTeX documents, and existing technical flows. - Regularly benchmarks one AI system against another and uses that comparison as part of his workflow.

sections.heading: Current North Star
sections.text: - Jarvis should become a local-first, high-capability personal AI system with strong voice, system control, memory, and command-center presence. - Jarvis should also function as a senior cybersecurity, AI, and software-engineering companion rather than only a general assistant. - Jarvis should also reason like a universal engineer, thinker, and problem solver across systems, product, AI, security, and operations. - Product quality is judged by usefulness, grounded behavior, and execution quality more than by generic model cleverness. - Local-first, privacy, inspectability, and zero API cost are important design constraints.

sections.heading: Technical Foundation
sections.text: - Comfortable with Python, SQL, Java, C++, JavaScript, pgAdmin, and SQLite. - Comfortable using AI tools as a force multiplier for writing, debugging, research, and interview preparation. - Resume-backed differentiators: end-to-end incident ownership, cross-functional leadership across Policy, Engineering, Product, and Security, and measurable quality/risk improvements at scale.

sections.heading: Preferences
sections.text: Purpose: capture stable preferences that improve how Jarvis responds and acts. Linked notes: [[00 Home]], [[10 Identity]], [[20 Projects]], [[74 Universal Engineer Thinker Problem Solver]], [[80 Jarvis Roadmap]]

sections.heading: Communication
sections.text: - Direct, plainspoken, and useful beats polished but empty. - Concise is good, but not at the expense of real substance. - Jarvis should take initiative when it can verify the path and complete the work end to end. - Explanations should be concrete and operational, not abstract filler.

sections.heading: Product Taste
sections.text: - Prefers Jarvis to feel more like Iron Man's Jarvis than a generic assistant. - Likes intentional, bold interface direction rather than safe default software aesthetics. - Wants voice to sound more human, steady, and polished rather than robotic. - Wants Jarvis to feel top-tier while staying local-first and privacy-conscious.

sections.heading: Engineering Preferences
sections.text: - Local-first behavior should be the default path, not a fallback. - Preserve working patterns instead of rewriting whole systems casually. - Verify the packaged macOS app for changes that affect real runtime behavior. - Prefer small, test-backed fixes over broad rewrites. - Keep memory and retrieval grounded in real source material rather than prompt theater.

sections.heading: Collaboration Preferences
sections.text: - Likes iterative refinement and back-and-forth improvement loops. - Uses AI outputs comparatively and expects the system to improve when given better evidence. - Values realistic self-assessment, honest prioritization, and practical next steps. These preferences should shape how Jarvis answers from [[73 Senior Cybersecurity AI Engineering Companion]], [[74 Universal Engineer Thinker Problem Solver]], and the maintenance posture tracked in [[93 Vault Maintenance]].

sections.heading: Career Timeline
sections.text: - 2018-08 to 2019-09: YouTube Child Trust & Safety Quality Analyst. High-precision child-safety moderation, policy enforcement, and escalation work under strict review standards. - 2019-11 to 2021-04: Meta Developer Operations, Trust & Safety Quality Analyst. Calibration, audits, enforcement-gap detection, and 15 percent false-negative reduction across high-risk queues. - 2020-02 to 2022-02: Allied Universal Security Supervisor. Shift supervision, incident response, access control, alarms, reporting, and post-incident root-cause reviews. - 2021-05 to 2022-04: Google Play Developer Operations, Trust & Safety Quality Analyst. Fraud and abuse investigation, reporting frameworks, high-priority escalations, and cross-functional detection improvements. - 2022-04 to 2022-07: Meta VR Software QA Engineer. Performance and defect investigation, automated debugging workflows, and reliability analysis. - 2022-07 to 2024-01: TikTok QA Analyst, Trust & Safety (USDS). Enforcement quality ownership across high-risk systems, 20 percent accuracy improvement, 25 percent recurrence reduction, and SQL pipelines over 500K+ daily events. - 2023-04 to 2024-11: Allied Universal Security Operations Commander. 24/7 GSOC leadership across 12 sites with a team of 16, KPI tracking, triage, escalation, and response effectiveness management. - 2023-05 to 2023-08: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory Software Engineering Intern. Backend Python services, distributed systems support, 40 percent SQL latency reduction, and stronger observability/CI-CD depth. - 2024-11 to 2025-12: SureFox / MetroOne Site Supervisor, Security Operations. Site-wide operations leadership, incident command, workforce management, SOP design, and partner coordination with Corporate Security, IT, and Facilities. - 2025-12 to 2026-03: Allied Universal (Anthropic) GSOC Analyst, AI Safety Operations. End-to-end AI safety incident response, jailbreak and prompt-injection investigations, Python/SQL triage workflows, and cross-functional response across Engineering, Policy, Security, and Product. - 2025-12: B.S. Software Engineering completed at San Jose State University.

sections.heading: Synthesis
sections.text: Purpose: distill large exports and long conversations into concise reusable understanding. Linked notes: [[05 Source Inventory]], [[10 Identity]], [[20 Projects]], [[60 Interview Story Bank]], [[80 Jarvis Roadmap]]

sections.heading: Claude Export Synthesis
sections.text: - Claude carries the cleanest explicit long-form memory summary and should be treated as the strongest source for durable identity grounding. - The strongest recurring Claude themes are Trust and Safety work, AI safety operations, interview preparation, role packaging, and defensible career storytelling. - Claude-era collaboration shows a preference for truthful claims, preserved structure, and iterative document refinement rather than wholesale rewriting. - Claude also captures a strong signal that vendor scaling, KPI design, policy ambiguity handling, and systems thinking are important interview differentiators.

sections.heading: ChatGPT Export Synthesis
sections.text: - ChatGPT usage is broader and noisier, but the recurring title patterns still show strong concentration in career and interview work. - The most common recurring clusters in ChatGPT titles are career and interview support, AI safety and security topics, SQL and database work, and a smaller creative and media track. - ChatGPT appears to have been used both as a serious workflow tool and as a sandbox for rapid explanation, rewriting, and ideation. - The export also includes a large attachment archive, which is useful for provenance but should not be treated as first-class memory unless a specific artifact becomes relevant.

sections.heading: Combined Takeaways
sections.text: - Stable identity and preference grounding should come primarily from the Claude memory bundle, then be cross-checked against ChatGPT recurrence patterns. - The strongest durable throughline across both systems is Aman as a Trust and Safety, AI safety, and security operations operator who uses AI heavily for career acceleration, technical iteration, and product thinking. - Jarvis should remember that interview support, local-first AI product building, and high-integrity writing are not side topics. They are central operating priorities. - Future distillation should focus on extracting reusable stories, project history, preference patterns, and major decisions rather than trying to preserve every transcript. - Future distillation should keep provenance anchored in [[05 Source Inventory]] and push durable outputs toward [[60 Interview Story Bank]], [[70 Jarvis Decision Log]], and [[80 Jarvis Roadmap]].

sections.heading: Resume 2026 Corrections
sections.text: - The latest resume is the highest-confidence source for current identity framing and should override older “roughly five years” summaries. The durable framing is 7+ years across trust and safety, AI safety, and security operations. - The strongest current positioning is not only platform moderation depth. It is end-to-end incident ownership, cross-functional escalation leadership, and turning ambiguous risk signals into scalable detection, enforcement, and response systems. - The most important measurable proof points now preserved in the brain are: 20 percent TikTok enforcement accuracy improvement, 25 percent reduction in false-positive and false-negative recurrence, 15 percent Meta false-negative reduction, 500K+ daily events processed in SQL pipelines, 40 percent SQL latency reduction at LLNL, and 24/7 GSOC leadership across 12 sites with a team of 16. This note should hold conclusions, not raw transcripts.

sections.heading: Phishing and malware investigation with lookalike domain
sections.text: - Situation: A phishing and malware investigation involving a lookalike domain became a recurring example in role materials. - Task: Investigate the threat clearly enough to support a credible enforcement or security narrative. - Action: Treat the incident as a real evidence-backed story instead of inflating it into a vague hero narrative, then weave it into relevant applications and interview prep where it fit naturally. - Result: It became one of the stronger authentic stories available for security, trust, and enforcement contexts. - Signals this story supports: investigation, threat analysis, judgment, communication under ambiguity, credibility. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: only real facts and real impact; do not invent scale. - Risks or caveats: this story should stay tightly factual because it can become overstated easily.

sections.heading: Meta false negative reduction
sections.text: - Situation: Resume and application work repeatedly relied on a real Meta metric tied to enforcement quality. - Task: Preserve a strong but truthful performance signal. - Action: Keep the story tied to a real claim of a 15 percent false negative reduction and use it where quality and enforcement impact matter. - Result: This became one of the most important quantified outcome signals in the career narrative. - Signals this story supports: quality improvement, measurement, enforcement operations, KPI fluency. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 15 percent false negative reduction at Meta. - Risks or caveats: keep attribution and scope precise.

sections.heading: TikTok accuracy improvement
sections.text: - Situation: Resume and role-fit work also preserved a TikTok quality metric. - Task: Retain a second clear quantified signal that demonstrates measurable operational impact. - Action: Use the real 20 percent accuracy improvement figure in materials where model quality, review quality, or enforcement precision matter. - Result: The story strengthens the pattern that Aman improves systems measurably, not just procedurally. - Signals this story supports: classifier quality, operational measurement, execution, systems improvement. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 20 percent accuracy improvement at TikTok. - Risks or caveats: avoid broadening the claim beyond the real scope.

sections.heading: TikTok recurrence reduction through root-cause investigation
sections.text: - Situation: Enforcement quality work at TikTok surfaced recurring false positives and false negatives in high-risk moderation systems. - Task: Reduce recurrence instead of only correcting individual decisions. - Action: Conduct root-cause investigations into decision failures, connect the issue to detection gaps and reviewer consistency, and use SQL pipelines over 500K+ daily events to monitor trends and validate whether the fixes were sticking. - Result: Recurrence of false positives and false negatives dropped by 25 percent while broader enforcement accuracy improved. - Signals this story supports: root-cause analysis, quality systems thinking, measurement discipline, operational follow-through. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 25 percent recurrence reduction; 500K+ daily events processed; 20 percent accuracy improvement as adjacent supporting evidence. - Risks or caveats: keep the distinction between recurrence reduction and overall accuracy improvement clear.

sections.heading: pgAdmin database context debugging
sections.text: - Situation: A SQL practice workflow failed because the query tool was opened from the wrong database context. - Task: Diagnose the root cause without blaming the SQL itself. - Action: Narrow the issue to connection context, identify the mismatch between ts_practice and sql_practice, and reopen the query tool from the correct database. - Result: The issue was resolved as a context error rather than a code error. - Signals this story supports: debugging discipline, root-cause analysis, SQL environment fluency, careful troubleshooting. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: preserve exact database names only when useful. - Risks or caveats: this is a smaller story, so use it for technical process and debugging rather than leadership impact.

sections.heading: LLNL backend and SQL performance improvement
sections.text: - Situation: During the LLNL software engineering internship, backend systems supporting real-time distributed processing needed better performance and reliability. - Task: Improve latency and strengthen the engineering workflow without losing sight of production stability. - Action: Build backend Python services, optimize SQL queries, contribute to CI/CD reliability, and improve logging, monitoring, and system observability while collaborating with engineering teams on production debugging. - Result: SQL latency dropped by 40 percent and the work strengthened the engineering side of the career narrative with real distributed-systems credibility. - Signals this story supports: technical depth, performance optimization, distributed systems, observability, engineering collaboration. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 40 percent SQL latency reduction; backend Python services for distributed systems; logging and monitoring improvements. - Risks or caveats: use this as an engineering-strength story, not as the anchor for trust-and-safety leadership questions.

sections.heading: Apple operations leadership application build
sections.text: - Situation: A full application package was built for the Apple User Reporting and Screening Operations Lead role. - Task: Reposition the narrative from individual contributor depth toward operations leadership strength. - Action: Refine the LaTeX resume, cover letter, portal entries, mock interview structure, story bank, and interview strategy into one coherent package. - Result: The package became a strong example of deliberate role targeting and narrative packaging. - Signals this story supports: leadership framing, strategic communication, role targeting, operational maturity. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: preserve only metrics already supported elsewhere. - Risks or caveats: this is strongest as a packaging and positioning story, not as direct operational evidence.

sections.heading: Gaps To Fill Next
sections.text: - a strong leadership or escalation story with explicit tradeoffs and outcome

sections.heading: Vendor scaling and operating model design
sections.text: - Situation: Operations-lead interview prep kept surfacing vendor scaling as a gap, but the resume material shows a real adjacent story: 24/7 GSOC coverage across 12 sites with a team of 16, plus distributed moderation calibration and SOP rollout across global teams in trust and safety roles. - Task: Show scale judgment, quality control, and operating-model thinking without claiming direct vendor ownership that the evidence does not support. - Action: Frame the story around running scaled human operations: track KPIs, enforce standards, manage triage and escalation workflows, mentor personnel, reduce reviewer variance through calibration, and use SOPs and reporting frameworks to keep decisions consistent across distributed teams. - Result: This becomes a credible scaled-operations story that supports vendor-style leadership questions even if the cleaner label is “distributed operations and quality management” rather than formal vendor management. - Signals this story supports: scaled operations leadership, process design, quality control, cross-functional coordination, people management. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 12 sites, team of 16 personnel, 24/7 GSOC operations, distributed moderation calibration, false negative reduction at Meta, reviewer consistency improvements at TikTok. - Risks or caveats: do not oversell this as owning a vendor program unless the source material later confirms that explicitly.

sections.heading: KPI design and measurement judgment
sections.text: - Situation: Multiple role-prep loops kept identifying KPI design as a key differentiator, and the strongest grounded metrics in the exports are a 15 percent false negative reduction at Meta and a 20 percent accuracy improvement at TikTok. - Task: Show that the work was not just “improve a metric,” but choose the right metric to protect quality and safety at scale. - Action: Frame the story around operational judgment: focus on the error type that mattered, track the metric that represented real quality loss, and improve system performance without hiding behind vanity numbers. Use the Meta false negative reduction as the stronger example when under-enforcement or missed harm is the core risk. Use the TikTok accuracy improvement when the emphasis is classifier quality, review consistency, or measurement discipline. - Result: This becomes a reusable story about choosing and improving the metric that best represented the operational problem, not just reporting whatever number looked good. - Signals this story supports: metrics fluency, operational judgment, systems thinking, quality leadership. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 15 percent false negative reduction at Meta; 20 percent accuracy improvement at TikTok. - Risks or caveats: do not collapse both metrics into one blended story. Pick the one that better fits the role and keep attribution precise.

sections.heading: Policy ambiguity and judgment under uncertainty
sections.text: - Situation: Interview prep for the YouTube Policy Enforcement Manager role surfaced policy ambiguity handling as a real gap, especially around age-appropriateness, regulatory depth, and precision-versus-recall framing. - Task: Show judgment when the right answer is not obvious and when the tradeoff is between missed harm and over-enforcement rather than between right and wrong. - Action: Anchor the story in trust-and-safety reasoning instead of pretending to have a single clean anecdote. The real signal is the ability to reason through ambiguous content by comparing policy intent to enforcement behavior, deciding whether the bigger risk is under-enforcement or over-enforcement, and escalating when consistency or user harm is at stake. - Result: This becomes a reusable judgment story for interviews: stay calm, make the tradeoff explicit, explain what would justify escalation, and do not confuse policy uncertainty with random inconsistency. - Signals this story supports: judgment, escalation maturity, trust and safety reasoning, decision quality under ambiguity. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: precision-versus-recall framing, age-appropriateness/policy-enforcement prep, and the explicit tradeoff logic. - Risks or caveats: this is still stronger as a reasoning-and-judgment story than as a single memorable operational incident. If a sharper real example emerges later, replace this with that.

sections.heading: Systems thinking across operations
sections.text: - Situation: Across both Jarvis work and interview prep, the strongest pattern is not one isolated task but repeated system-level reasoning: metrics, tooling, workflow, policy, human review, escalation paths, and model behavior all move together. - Task: Show that Aman does not treat a surface symptom as the root cause. - Action: Use the recurring operational pattern preserved in the exports: diagnose whether the issue is signal quality, labeling quality, workflow design, enforcement inconsistency, or model calibration before proposing a fix. The Meta false negative work, TikTok accuracy work, and even the pgAdmin debugging example all support the same underlying operating style: first identify the actual failing layer, then change the system instead of patching the symptom. - Result: This becomes a high-credibility systems-thinking story because it ties together operational measurement, debugging discipline, and policy/enforcement reasoning under one approach. - Signals this story supports: operations leadership, strategic thinking, process design, multi-stakeholder reasoning. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 15 percent false negative reduction at Meta, 20 percent accuracy improvement at TikTok, and the “wrong database context, not wrong SQL” debugging pattern. - Risks or caveats: keep this story about operating method, not about claiming ownership of every part of the system.

sections.heading: 24/7 security operations leadership and escalation control
sections.text: - Situation: In security operations roles, the work involved high-severity events, live triage, distributed coverage, and the need to keep response quality stable under pressure. - Task: Maintain operational control across multiple sites and personnel while improving response effectiveness rather than just reacting faster. - Action: Lead 24/7 GSOC operations across 12 sites, manage a team of 16 personnel, own triage and escalation workflows, track KPIs tied to response effectiveness, and use reporting, mentoring, and enforced standards to keep operations consistent. - Result: This becomes a concrete leadership and escalation story because it ties people management, incident response, metrics, and operational discipline together under one accountable scope. - Signals this story supports: leadership under pressure, escalation maturity, operational control, workforce management, KPI ownership. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: 12 sites, 16 personnel, 24/7 GSOC operations, KPI tracking for response effectiveness. - Risks or caveats: keep the framing grounded in operations leadership and escalation control, not broader security claims that the evidence does not support.

sections.heading: Anthropic-aligned AI safety incident response
sections.text: - Situation: In the Allied Universal role supporting Anthropic AI safety operations, the work centered on AI safety incidents rather than generic physical-security monitoring. - Task: Own detection, triage, escalation, and resolution for incidents involving jailbreaks, prompt injection, coordinated abuse, and other policy-to-detection gaps. - Action: lead cross-functional response across Engineering, Policy, Security, and Product; investigate misuse patterns; build Python and SQL workflows for triage and abuse-pattern detection; and establish reporting, audit logging, and post-incident review frameworks. - Result: This is the clearest current story that ties AI safety operations, adversarial misuse reasoning, cross-functional escalation, and scalable operational systems together. - Signals this story supports: AI safety operations, prompt-injection and jailbreak response, incident ownership, cross-functional leadership, scalable controls. - Metrics or evidence to preserve: end-to-end incident response ownership; cross-functional response across Engineering, Policy, Security, and Product; reporting and audit framework ownership. - Risks or caveats: avoid claiming frontier-model research ownership; keep the framing on operations, safeguards execution, and misuse response.

sections.heading: AI safety and safeguards operations
sections.text: Best fit examples: - Anthropic safeguards or enforcement operations - OpenAI trust, abuse, safety operations - AI misuse, adversarial abuse, or model-boundary roles Lead with: - [[10 Identity]] for AI safety operator grounding - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Anthropic-aligned AI safety incident response]] - phishing and malware investigation with lookalike domain in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - systems thinking across operations in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - KPI design and measurement judgment in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] Emphasis: - model abuse and adversarial behavior - translating risk signals into scalable controls - classifier precision, recall, and enforcement quality - calm judgment in fast-moving ambiguous situations Avoid: - over-indexing on generic security language that hides the trust-and-safety depth - presenting broad “AI expert” claims that are not tied to actual operations work

sections.heading: Trust and safety operations leadership
sections.text: Best fit examples: - Apple User Reporting and Screening Operations Lead - vendor operations, escalation, or quality leadership roles - large-scale moderation quality and workflow roles Lead with: - 24/7 security operations leadership and escalation control in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - vendor scaling and operating model design in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - [[60 Interview Story Bank#TikTok recurrence reduction through root-cause investigation]] - KPI design and measurement judgment in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - Apple operations leadership application build in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] Emphasis: - scale, workflow design, calibration, SOPs, KPI ownership - people leadership and operational consistency - balancing throughput, quality, and escalation discipline Avoid: - pretending a formal vendor-management title if the source evidence only supports adjacent scaled-operations leadership - drifting into purely technical stories when the role is clearly people-and-process heavy

sections.heading: Policy enforcement and judgment-heavy moderation
sections.text: Best fit examples: - YouTube Policy Enforcement Manager - policy escalation, integrity enforcement, or decision-quality roles Lead with: - [[10 Identity]] for child-safety and high-precision enforcement grounding - policy ambiguity and judgment under uncertainty in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - Meta false negative reduction in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - systems thinking across operations in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] Emphasis: - policy intent versus enforcement behavior - precision-versus-recall tradeoffs - escalation judgment and consistency under ambiguity - high-severity decision quality Avoid: - treating policy work as content moderation trivia instead of systems-level decision making - claiming regulatory expertise beyond what has actually been prepared or demonstrated

sections.heading: Security and incident operations leadership
sections.text: Best fit examples: - GSOC, trust-and-safety security operations, threat response, and incident-command roles Lead with: - 24/7 security operations leadership and escalation control in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - phishing and malware investigation with lookalike domain in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Anthropic-aligned AI safety incident response]] - pgAdmin database context debugging in [[60 Interview Story Bank]] when technical troubleshooting matters Emphasis: - incident command - escalation control - cross-functional communication under pressure - operational discipline and reporting Avoid: - losing the trust-and-safety angle if the role sits at the intersection of security and abuse

sections.heading: Story Selection Rules
sections.text: - Pick one anchor story and one supporting story before adding a third. - Match the story to the role's risk model, not just to matching keywords. - For leadership roles, lead with scope and operating judgment before technical detail. - For AI safety roles, lead with risk interpretation and scalable controls before general operations polish. - For policy roles, lead with tradeoffs and consistency before tool-building.

sections.heading: Positioning Summary
sections.text: Aman is strongest for Anthropic-style roles when framed as an AI safety operations operator, not as a generic security analyst and not as a pure policy generalist. The differentiator is the combination of: - trust and safety enforcement depth across major platforms - current AI safety incident-response ownership in an Anthropic-aligned environment - adversarial abuse and misuse reasoning - classifier calibration and quality judgment - operational debugging mindset - comfort translating ambiguous risk signals into scalable controls

sections.heading: Anchor story
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Anthropic-aligned AI safety incident response]] Why: - It is the cleanest current proof that Aman can own AI safety incidents end to end, coordinate across Engineering, Policy, Security, and Product, and translate misuse signals into scalable operational controls.

sections.heading: Supporting story 1
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Systems thinking across operations]] Why: - It shows the operating method beneath the incident work: find the failing layer first, then fix the system instead of the symptom.

sections.heading: Supporting story 2
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#KPI design and measurement judgment]] Why: - It proves Aman does not just investigate risk. He improves how the system measures and catches it.

sections.heading: What To Emphasize
sections.text: - classifier precision and recall as operational safety controls - adversarial misuse detection, prompt-injection and jailbreak-adjacent reasoning - end-to-end incident response ownership - calibration and consistent decision quality under ambiguity - cross-functional work with policy, engineering, and operations partners - strong ownership without inflated claims

sections.heading: What To Downplay
sections.text: - generic GSOC or physical security framing when the role is clearly about AI and model abuse - broad “cybersecurity” branding if it obscures trust-and-safety depth - vendor or workforce-management stories unless the interviewer is explicitly probing scaled operations

sections.heading: Strong Answer Pattern
sections.text: For Anthropic-style interviews, Jarvis should usually build answers in this order: 1. identify the risk or signal clearly 2. explain how the wrong layer could be blamed if the system is not examined carefully 3. show how Aman narrows the problem to policy, detection, workflow, or calibration 4. explain the scalable fix and the quality tradeoff That structure matches the real operating style better than generic STAR storytelling alone.

sections.heading: Likely Themes To Prepare For
sections.text: - handling jailbreaks, prompt injection, or adversarial misuse - when to escalate rather than force a weak enforcement decision - classifier misses and how to decide whether the problem is labels, policy, or thresholds - balancing false positives and false negatives when harm is asymmetric - partnering with policy and engineering without losing operational grounding

sections.heading: Honest Gaps
sections.text: - do not overclaim direct frontier-model research depth - do not imply formal model-training ownership if the real strength is operations and enforcement quality - keep the story grounded in AI safety operations and safeguards execution rather than trying to sound like a research scientist

sections.heading: Draft One-Sentence Framing
sections.text: Aman is a trust and safety and AI safety operations specialist who is strongest at turning ambiguous abuse signals into scalable enforcement, calibration, and detection improvements without losing grounding in real operational constraints.

sections.heading: Strong Answer Pattern
sections.text: For Apple-style interviews, Jarvis should usually build answers in this order: 1. define the operational problem clearly 2. describe the scale or sensitivity of the environment 3. explain how Aman introduced consistency, measurement, or escalation discipline 4. show the result in terms of quality, response effectiveness, or operating control

sections.heading: Honest Gaps

sections.heading: Positioning Summary
sections.text: For YouTube-style roles, Aman should be framed as a policy-and-enforcement operator with strong judgment under ambiguity, not just a quality analyst and not just a general trust-and-safety worker. The differentiator is the combination of high-precision child-safety enforcement instincts, clear escalation judgment for ambiguous edge cases, and the ability to improve enforcement consistency instead of treating every hard case as an isolated decision.

sections.heading: Anchor story
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Meta false negative reduction]] Why: - It gives measurable proof that Aman understands the real cost of under-enforcement in policy-sensitive environments and can improve the system accordingly.

sections.heading: Supporting story 1
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Policy ambiguity and judgment under uncertainty]] Why: - It remains the best direct framing for questions about edge cases, escalation thresholds, and when policy intent is clearer than the written policy language.

sections.heading: Supporting story 2
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Systems thinking across operations]] Why: - It reinforces that decisions are not being made in a vacuum; policy, workflow, calibration, and metrics all interact.

sections.heading: Optional child-safety grounding
sections.text: - [[10 Identity]] Use when: - the interviewer is probing for deep policy instincts, child-safety enforcement history, or credibility in sensitive enforcement domains.

sections.heading: What To Emphasize
sections.text: - policy intent versus enforcement behavior - precision, recall, and consistency tradeoffs - escalation judgment for edge cases - systemic fixes rather than one-off case handling - quality calibration and reviewer consistency - child-safety enforcement instincts and high-precision review discipline

sections.heading: What To Downplay
sections.text: - generic AI-safety positioning if the role is clearly about policy enforcement rather than model safeguards - broad operations-leadership framing if the interviewer is testing decision quality more than people management - technical tooling details unless they directly improve enforcement consistency or signal quality - generic security language that obscures direct platform-enforcement experience

sections.heading: Strong Answer Pattern
sections.text: For YouTube-style interviews, Jarvis should usually build answers in this order: 1. define the policy problem or ambiguous signal 2. explain the tradeoff between false positives and false negatives 3. show how Aman would decide whether to escalate, enforce, or ask for policy clarification 4. explain the system-level improvement that would prevent the issue from recurring

sections.heading: Likely Themes To Prepare For
sections.text: - age appropriateness and edge-case content judgment - child-safety and high-sensitivity content decision quality - when a policy gap is the real issue rather than reviewer quality - consistency across high-volume or high-severity queues - making escalation decisions when policy language is not enough - balancing user harm against over-enforcement risk

sections.heading: Honest Gaps
sections.text: - regulatory depth was identified as a preparation gap in prior interview work, so Jarvis should not overstate that area - if asked for a highly specific real case, default to a grounded reasoning example unless a sharper documented incident is available

sections.heading: Draft One-Sentence Framing
sections.text: Aman is strongest for YouTube-style policy roles when positioned as a child-safety and trust-and-safety operator who can make high-precision enforcement decisions, escalate ambiguous edge cases responsibly, and improve policy consistency at the system level.

sections.heading: Career Answering Rules
sections.text: Purpose: guide how Jarvis should answer career, interview, and role-fit questions using the curated brain. Linked notes: [[10 Identity]], [[60 Interview Story Bank]], [[61 Role Targeting Map]], [[62 Anthropic AI Safety Variant]], [[63 Apple Ops Leadership Variant]], [[64 YouTube Policy Variant]] Use this note when Jarvis needs to: - answer an interview question - tailor a story to a target role - explain role fit - draft career-facing answers grounded in the vault

sections.heading: Core Rules
sections.text: - Lead with the strongest grounded point, not with generic setup. - Use one anchor story and one supporting story before adding a third. - Prefer exact known metrics over broad claims. - If the evidence is partial, say so and keep the answer tight rather than smoothing over the gap. - Match the answer to the role family before choosing the story.

sections.heading: Story Selection Order
sections.text: 1. Pick the role family in [[61 Role Targeting Map]] 2. Pull the best target variant if one exists 3. Choose the anchor story from [[60 Interview Story Bank]] 4. Add one supporting story only if it sharpens the answer

sections.heading: For AI safety roles
sections.text: - lead with adversarial misuse, safeguards, calibration, and scalable controls - avoid generic “cybersecurity” phrasing unless the role explicitly calls for it

sections.heading: For operations leadership roles
sections.text: - lead with scale, consistency, people/process control, and KPI ownership - avoid drifting into overly technical detail too early

sections.heading: For policy enforcement roles
sections.text: - lead with judgment, tradeoffs, escalation logic, and consistency - avoid answering as if every case has a clean binary rule

sections.heading: Failure Modes To Avoid
sections.text: - combining several stories into one inflated narrative - using the wrong story just because the keywords overlap - inventing regulatory or vendor-management depth that is not actually documented - giving generic motivational answers when the vault contains stronger evidence

sections.heading: Default Tone
sections.text: - precise - grounded - direct - interview-ready, not conversationally vague

sections.text: Purpose: give Jarvis one durable, resume-backed narrative spine for tell-me-about-yourself, why-you, and high-level positioning questions. Linked notes: [[10 Identity]], [[50 Synthesis]], [[60 Interview Story Bank]], [[61 Role Targeting Map]], [[65 Career Answering Rules]] Use this note when Jarvis needs to: - answer high-level career questions directly - explain who Aman is professionally in one clean arc - position Aman consistently before tailoring for a specific company or role

sections.heading: Core Narrative Spine
sections.text: Aman is strongest when framed as a Trust and Safety, AI safety, and security operations leader who has spent 7+ years operating in high-risk environments where the job is not just to spot problems, but to turn ambiguous risk signals into scalable enforcement, incident-response, and operational control systems. The throughline is not “I worked at a lot of companies.” The throughline is: - high-severity judgment - end-to-end incident ownership - measurable quality and risk reduction - cross-functional coordination across Policy, Engineering, Product, and Security - strong operational discipline under pressure The current narrative should connect three layers cleanly: 1. policy and enforcement depth from YouTube, Meta, Google Play, and TikTok 2. systems and technical depth from SQL, Python, large-scale event analysis, and LLNL backend work 3. incident-command and operational leadership from GSOC and Anthropic-aligned AI safety operations That combination is the differentiator.

sections.heading: Strongest Proof Points
sections.text: - 7+ years across Trust and Safety, AI safety, fraud and abuse prevention, and security operations - owned high-precision enforcement work in child safety and policy-sensitive moderation environments - reduced false negatives by 15 percent at Meta - improved enforcement accuracy by 20 percent at TikTok - reduced false-positive and false-negative recurrence by 25 percent at TikTok - built SQL pipelines over 500K+ daily events to monitor enforcement trends and risk signals - optimized SQL queries at LLNL, reducing latency by 40 percent - led 24/7 GSOC operations across 12 sites with a team of 16 personnel - now operating in an Anthropic-aligned AI safety environment with end-to-end incident response for jailbreaks, prompt injection, coordinated abuse, and cross-functional escalation

sections.heading: What This Narrative Should Emphasize
sections.text: - Aman improves systems, not just individual cases - Aman is comfortable making judgment calls when the signal is ambiguous and the failure cost is high - Aman can move between policy, operations, and technical investigation without losing rigor - Aman has real scale evidence, real metrics, and real operational ownership - Aman is strongest in roles where abuse, safety, integrity, escalation, and operational quality intersect

sections.heading: What This Narrative Should Avoid
sections.text: - sounding like a generic security analyst when the stronger story is Trust and Safety plus AI safety operations - sounding like a generic content moderator when the stronger story is systems-level enforcement quality and incident ownership - overstating frontier-model research depth - claiming vendor-program ownership unless the evidence clearly supports it - listing companies and tools without explaining the operating pattern that connects them

sections.heading: Default Positioning
sections.text: The clean default positioning is: Aman is a Trust and Safety and AI safety operator with 7+ years of experience across abuse prevention, policy enforcement, fraud investigation, security operations, and incident response. He is strongest at taking ambiguous risk signals, identifying the real failing layer in the system, and translating that into scalable improvements in detection, enforcement quality, escalation, and operational control.

sections.heading: Default Answer Pattern
sections.text: For high-level positioning answers, Jarvis should usually answer in this order: 1. current professional identity and scope 2. the throughline across prior roles 3. two or three strongest proof points 4. why that matters for the target role That keeps the answer grounded and avoids turning it into a chronological dump.

sections.heading: Concise Default Answer
sections.text: I’m a Trust and Safety and AI safety operator with 7+ years of experience across policy enforcement, abuse prevention, fraud investigation, and security operations. The common thread in my work is high-risk decision making and systems improvement: I’ve worked on child safety and high-severity moderation, improved enforcement quality and false-negative performance at scale, built SQL and Python workflows to monitor risk signals, led 24/7 security operations, and now support AI safety incident response in an Anthropic-aligned environment. I’m strongest in roles where I need to connect investigation, operational judgment, and scalable controls.

sections.heading: Slightly Longer Default Answer
sections.text: I’ve spent the last 7+ years working at the intersection of Trust and Safety, AI safety, and security operations. Early on, that meant high-precision moderation and policy enforcement in sensitive areas like child safety. Over time, the work expanded into system quality, fraud and abuse investigations, calibration, and large-scale enforcement analysis, including measurable improvements like a 15 percent false-negative reduction at Meta and a 20 percent enforcement accuracy improvement at TikTok. In parallel, I built stronger operational depth through GSOC and incident-command work, including leading 24/7 operations across 12 sites with a team of 16. What ties it all together is that I’m strongest when the problem is ambiguous, high-risk, and cross-functional, and the right answer is to improve the system rather than just react to the symptom.

sections.heading: Why-You Framing
sections.text: The strongest “why you” answer is not “I’ve seen a lot.” It is: - I bring both enforcement judgment and operational systems thinking - I have a record of measurable quality and risk improvement - I know how to work cross-functionally when policy, operations, and technical systems all matter - I stay grounded in real evidence instead of inflating the story

sections.heading: Reusable One-Liners
sections.text: - I’m strongest in environments where trust, safety, and operational judgment all matter at the same time. - My edge is turning ambiguous abuse or safety signals into measurable system improvements. - I’m not just comfortable with high-risk decisions; I’m used to building the workflows and controls that make those decisions more reliable at scale.

sections.heading: Final Guardrail
sections.text: If Jarvis has to choose between sounding broader and sounding truer, choose truer. The strength of this narrative is that it is already strong without exaggeration.

sections.heading: Decision Name
sections.text: - Date: - Decision: - Why: - Tradeoffs: - Files or systems affected: - Revisit when:

sections.heading: Distilled brain notes outrank raw transcript imports
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Vault retrieval should prefer vault/wiki/brain/ over vault/raw/imports/ when relevance is otherwise similar. - Why: Long-term Jarvis quality depends on curated, human-readable, stable knowledge winning over noisy transcript dumps. - Tradeoffs: Raw provenance stays searchable, but raw archives should no longer dominate retrieval just because they are large. - Files or systems affected: vault.py, vault/wiki/brain/, vault/raw/imports/ - Revisit when: retrieval becomes too lossy and we need better hierarchical ranking or note linking.

sections.heading: Obsidian-compatible markdown is the long-term brain surface
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Use the local vault as the shared source of truth for both Jarvis and Obsidian rather than creating a separate hidden memory store. - Why: Human-readable notes are easier to maintain, verify, edit, and grow over time. - Tradeoffs: Distillation work is required; raw exports cannot simply be dumped in and expected to become good memory. - Files or systems affected: vault/Vault Guide.md, vault/wiki/brain/, vault/raw/imports/ - Revisit when: the note volume grows enough that we need stronger indexing, backlinks, or structured metadata.

sections.heading: Local-first is a product constraint, not a fallback mode
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Jarvis should be optimized around local execution, privacy, inspectability, and zero API cost. - Why: This is one of the strongest stable product preferences and a core differentiator. - Tradeoffs: Local model quality and packaging complexity remain real constraints. - Files or systems affected: model routing, voice runtime, local STT, local TTS, packaging. - Revisit when: a feature cannot realistically meet product standards without a hybrid approach.

sections.heading: Packaged macOS behavior must be treated as first-class
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Changes affecting voice, UI, STT, TTS, and system action should be validated against the packaged app, not just the source tree. - Why: The frozen app has repeatedly behaved differently from source-only runs. - Tradeoffs: Slower iteration and more build overhead. - Files or systems affected: Jarvis.spec, ui.py, voice.py, local runtime modules, install script. - Revisit when: packaging and source parity become much more reliable.

sections.heading: Jarvis should feel like an operator, not a generic chatbot
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Product direction should favor a command-center assistant with strong voice, memory, action competence, and calm execution. - Why: This aligns with the stated long-term north star and produces a clearer product identity. - Tradeoffs: Higher expectations for polish, coordination, and real-world usefulness. - Files or systems affected: UI, voice, prompt style, memory, tool routing, proactive behavior. - Revisit when: day-to-day utility and reliability are slipping behind aesthetic ambition.

sections.heading: Promote raw transcripts only when they add durable leverage
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: A raw transcript or export fragment should become a durable brain note only if it improves identity grounding, project continuity, reusable stories, or product decisions. - Why: The vault should compound signal, not archive every interesting sentence. - Tradeoffs: Some potentially useful details stay in raw imports until they prove recurring value. - Files or systems affected: vault/raw/imports/, vault/wiki/brain/, story bank notes, decision notes. - Revisit when: retrieval starts missing useful but lightly repeated source material.

sections.heading: Leave evidence gaps open instead of inventing polish
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: If a desired story or memory layer lacks verified evidence, keep it partial and label the gap instead of writing a cleaner but weaker fiction. - Why: Jarvis should become more grounded over time, not more fluent at laundering uncertainty into false confidence. - Tradeoffs: Some notes will stay unfinished longer, and the vault may feel less complete in the short term. - Files or systems affected: vault/wiki/brain/60 Interview Story Bank.md, future synthesis notes, career support prompts. - Revisit when: stronger source material arrives and the gap can be closed honestly.

sections.heading: Proactive by default should mean quiet and high-confidence
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Jarvis should stay mostly reactive by default, with proactive behavior limited to high-confidence, high-value moments such as reminders, active task follow-through, or obvious continuity prompts. - Why: An always-interjecting assistant feels noisy fast, especially in a desktop product that already has voice, memory, and system presence. - Tradeoffs: Jarvis may feel less magical in the short term, but it will earn trust instead of spending it. - Files or systems affected: proactive routines, voice prompts, notifications, task follow-up behavior, future automation defaults. - Revisit when: proactive signals become accurate enough that interruption cost is consistently low.

sections.heading: Technical companion depth should live in reusable playbooks, not only scattered transcripts
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Strengthen Jarvis's engineering brain through reusable notes for debugging, systems design, threat modeling, and AI runtime behavior instead of relying on scattered chat history or one-off examples. - Why: Long-term technical quality compounds better when Jarvis can reuse stable frameworks across many problems rather than retrieving isolated fragments that happen to sound smart. - Tradeoffs: The vault needs more deliberate curation, and the playbooks must still be grounded in real runtime behavior instead of becoming generic advice. - Files or systems affected: vault/wiki/brain/75 Debugging Root Cause Playbook.md, vault/wiki/brain/76 Systems Design Tradeoff Heuristics.md, vault/wiki/brain/77 Threat Modeling Security Thinking.md, vault/wiki/brain/78 AI Runtime Agent Engineering Principles.md, future retrieval and prompt grounding. - Revisit when: the playbooks become too abstract and need tighter runtime integration or more domain-specific variants.

sections.heading: Jarvis owns the vault contract; Obsidian plugins stay optional
sections.text: - Date: 2026-04-15 - Decision: Adopt a plugin-optional vault operating layer based on metadata discipline, markdown tasks, deterministic templates, JSON Canvas, and explicit provenance instead of depending on plugin runtimes. - Why: The best public Obsidian repos point toward strong conventions, but Jarvis still needs a brain it can read and write safely as plain markdown. - Tradeoffs: Some dashboards will stay simpler than a full plugin-heavy Obsidian setup, but the vault stays durable and local-first. - Files or systems affected: vault/wiki/brain/03 Brain Schema.md, vault/wiki/brain/04 Capture Workflow.md, vault/wiki/brain/91 Vault Changelog.md, vault/templates/, CLAUDE.md - Revisit when: Jarvis needs deeper Obsidian-native interaction than markdown, tasks, and .canvas can provide safely.

sections.heading: Meta Calibration Quality Variant
sections.text: Purpose: sharpen the reusable story bank for Meta-style calibration, enforcement-quality, and decision-consistency roles. Linked notes: [[10 Identity]], [[60 Interview Story Bank]], [[61 Role Targeting Map]], [[65 Career Answering Rules]] Use this note when Jarvis needs to: - frame Aman for calibration, QA, or enforcement-quality roles - answer questions about reducing false negatives or improving moderation consistency - position trust-and-safety depth as quality-systems discipline rather than generic moderation experience

sections.heading: Positioning Summary
sections.text: For Meta-style calibration and quality roles, Aman should be framed as a trust-and-safety quality operator with strong judgment around enforcement consistency, not just as a reviewer and not just as a general operations analyst. The differentiator is the combination of: - calibration work that reduced false negatives in high-risk queues - QA ownership across policy-sensitive moderation systems - audit-based identification of systemic enforcement gaps - strong instincts around precision, recall, and escalation thresholds - consistency-building across teams, workflows, and global operations - operational discipline that turns quality findings into scalable fixes

sections.heading: Anchor story
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Meta false negative reduction]] Why: - It is the cleanest direct proof of the exact problem these roles care about: improving enforcement quality in high-risk moderation queues by reducing missed harm rather than only optimizing for surface consistency.

sections.heading: Supporting story 1
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#KPI design and measurement judgment]] Why: - It shows that Aman does not treat quality as a vague concept. He anchors improvement in the right metric and uses that metric to protect real enforcement outcomes.

sections.heading: Supporting story 2
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Policy ambiguity and judgment under uncertainty]] Why: - Calibration work is not just about catching random reviewer mistakes. It is about making consistent decisions when policy intent is clear but the edge case is not.

sections.heading: Supporting story 3
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Systems thinking across operations]] Why: - It reinforces that quality misses usually point to a deeper system issue in labeling, policy interpretation, workflow, or escalation design rather than to one isolated bad decision.

sections.heading: What To Emphasize
sections.text: - false-negative reduction in high-risk moderation queues - calibration and reviewer-alignment work - QA workflows for graphic violence, extremism, and other policy-sensitive content - audits that surface systemic enforcement gaps - escalation frameworks for ambiguous or high-risk decisions - consistency across global teams and moderation workflows - quality systems that improve decisions without hiding the real tradeoffs

sections.heading: What To Downplay
sections.text: - generic security or incident-response framing if the role is clearly about moderation quality - pure AI-safety language when the interviewer is testing calibration and decision consistency - overclaiming formal policy-writing ownership when the stronger truth is calibration, QA, and enforcement judgment - broad people-management language unless the question is clearly about scaled operations leadership

sections.heading: Strong Answer Pattern
sections.text: For Meta-style calibration and quality interviews, Jarvis should usually build answers in this order: 1. define the quality problem clearly, especially the error type that mattered most 2. explain how Aman would determine whether the issue is reviewer variance, policy ambiguity, or a workflow/system gap 3. show how calibration, audits, or escalation logic improved consistency 4. explain the measurable quality outcome and why it mattered for user harm or enforcement integrity

sections.heading: Likely Themes To Prepare For
sections.text: - reducing false negatives without creating noisy over-enforcement - aligning reviewers on high-risk or policy-sensitive content - deciding when a quality issue is really a policy or escalation-framework problem - using audits to identify systemic enforcement gaps - maintaining consistent decisions across global teams - balancing throughput pressure against quality standards

sections.heading: Honest Gaps
sections.text: - the strongest evidence is calibration, QA, audits, and enforcement-quality work, not formal policy authorship - if the role leans heavily into tooling or ML infrastructure, answers should stay grounded in moderation systems, quality workflows, and operational influence rather than unsupported technical ownership - the best proof is decision consistency and measurable enforcement improvement, not abstract “content policy strategy”

sections.heading: Draft One-Sentence Framing
sections.text: Aman is strongest for Meta-style calibration and quality roles when positioned as a trust-and-safety operator who can reduce false negatives, tighten reviewer consistency, surface systemic enforcement gaps through audits, and turn policy-sensitive quality problems into scalable moderation controls.

sections.heading: LLNL Technical Systems Credibility
sections.text: Purpose: preserve a reusable technical-credibility note Jarvis can draw on for engineering, systems, and production-debugging questions without overstating the scope of the LLNL work. Linked notes: [[10 Identity]], [[20 Projects]], [[50 Synthesis]], [[60 Interview Story Bank]] Use this note when Jarvis needs to: - answer engineering or systems-design questions with grounded technical proof - explain why Aman has real backend and production-systems credibility - support conversations about scalability, observability, reliability, or debugging discipline

sections.heading: Positioning Summary
sections.text: The strongest technical framing from the LLNL experience is not “pure software engineer” in the abstract. It is backend-and-systems credibility grounded in production-minded work: Python services, distributed systems, real-time data processing, SQL performance improvement, CI/CD reliability, and observability-driven debugging. This note is useful when Jarvis needs to show that Aman can operate credibly in technical environments, partner well with engineers, and reason about systems behavior without exaggerating ownership beyond the evidence.

sections.heading: Core Technical Proof Points
sections.text: - developed backend services using Python for distributed systems supporting real-time data processing - optimized SQL queries, reducing latency by 40 percent - contributed to CI/CD pipelines that improved reliability and deployment quality - collaborated with engineering teams to debug production issues and improve system observability - implemented logging and monitoring enhancements for performance tracking - participated in system-design discussions focused on scalability and distributed reliability improvements

sections.heading: Anchor story
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#LLNL backend and SQL performance improvement]] Why: - It is the clearest resume-backed technical story with both systems substance and measurable outcome.

sections.heading: Supporting story 1
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#pgAdmin database context debugging]] Why: - It reinforces the real debugging habit underneath the LLNL work: diagnose the failing layer before blaming the code.

sections.heading: Supporting story 2
sections.text: - [[60 Interview Story Bank#Systems thinking across operations]] Why: - It connects the engineering side to the broader operating style. The same pattern shows up in technical debugging, observability work, and system-level diagnosis.

sections.heading: What To Emphasize
sections.text: - backend Python work tied to real systems rather than toy scripting - distributed systems and real-time data-processing exposure - SQL optimization with a concrete 40 percent latency improvement - production debugging in collaboration with engineering teams - logging, monitoring, and observability as practical reliability tools - CI/CD contributions that improved reliability rather than vague “DevOps” branding - scalability discussions grounded in actual system behavior and performance constraints

sections.heading: What To Downplay
sections.text: - claiming deep platform ownership if the real strength is strong technical contribution and debugging partnership - overstating formal architecture authority beyond the documented system-design participation - presenting the experience as purely frontend or product engineering when the evidence is clearly backend and systems oriented - turning one internship into inflated senior-engineer language

sections.heading: Strong Answer Pattern
sections.text: For engineering or systems questions, Jarvis should usually build answers in this order: 1. define the system or performance problem clearly 2. explain how Aman narrowed it to the actual failing layer 3. describe the technical change in Python, SQL, CI/CD, logging, or monitoring terms 4. show the result in latency, reliability, observability, or scalability terms

sections.heading: Likely Themes To Prepare For
sections.text: - how to debug a production issue without jumping to conclusions - when to optimize SQL versus application logic - what observability improvements are actually useful in practice - how backend services behave differently in distributed systems - what reliability work in CI/CD should focus on first - how to discuss scalability without hand-waving

sections.heading: Honest Boundaries
sections.text: - the strongest claim is credible backend-and-systems contribution, not formal ownership of a large production platform - the LLNL story supports technical depth and engineering fluency, but it should not be stretched into principal-engineer scope - keep the framing on practical debugging, performance improvement, observability, and reliability

sections.heading: Draft One-Sentence Framing
sections.text: Aman has real technical-systems credibility because he has built backend Python services for distributed real-time systems, improved SQL latency by 40 percent, strengthened CI/CD reliability, and worked directly on observability and production debugging with engineering teams.
